full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This past week, on St. Patrick's Day,<|4.10|> <|5.04|> I revisited one of my favorite bits of Irish wisdom.<|9.14|> <|10.02|> I don't know if it's a blessing or a toast.<|13.74|> <|13.74|> With the Irish, I think those may be fully interchangeable.<|17.70|> <|17.70|> And certainly in my family of origin, that is true.<|21.12|> <|22.46|> It goes like this.<|23.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.08|> May you never forget what is worth remembering,<|3.86|> <|3.86|> nor ever remember what is best forgotten.<|6.54|> <|8.34|> May you never forget what is worth remembering,<|12.70|> <|13.70|> nor ever remember what is best forgotten.<|17.60|> <|19.08|> This sums up beautifully and succinctly<|22.98|> <|22.98|> the promise from God in our Jeremiah reading this morning.<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.46|> For I will forgive their iniquity<|3.74|> <|4.58|> and remember their sin no more,<|7.28|> <|8.44|> not remembering what is best forgotten.<|11.60|> <|13.24|> And this has been with me all week,<|16.30|> <|16.30|> this notion of intentional forgetting,<|20.08|> <|20.98|> this practice of letting go of that<|24.64|> <|24.64|> which causes us sadness,<|26.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> pain, unease, laying down the memories that plague us, things done to us, things we've done, things done and left undone.<|17.20|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.04|> This proclamation from Jeremiah that God will not only forgive their iniquity, but will also<|9.70|> <|9.70|> remember their sin no more, is both thrilling and confounding. Memory is so important to us, isn't<|20.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.04|> How else do we know ourselves?<|2.74|> <|3.88|> John Locke put forth that our very identities exist only because of our memories.<|11.40|> <|12.62|> One of the reasons that cognitive issues distress us so is that we wonder who we are if we forget.<|24.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> if we forget people and events from our life.<|5.00|> <|7.50|> If we're honest, there's also a part of ourselves<|11.28|> <|11.28|> that doesn't want to forget wrongs done to us.<|15.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> If that were not so, we wouldn't have so many warnings<|5.00|> <|6.08|> and witticisms.<|8.02|> <|8.02|> Forgive, but do not forget.<|11.26|> <|11.26|> Fool me once, shame on you.<|14.00|> <|14.00|> Fool me twice, shame on me.<|16.60|> <|17.56|> And my personal favorite,<|19.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.84|> Some forgive and forget,<|3.34|> <|4.18|> more forgive and remember,<|7.10|> <|7.10|> most forgive and remind.<|10.50|> <|13.90|> All too often,<|15.56|> <|17.04|> our motivations for not forgetting<|20.20|> <|21.18|> are simply trying to avoid further pain<|24.84|> <|24.84|> or keeping score,<|26.88|> <|26.88|> or it's about our ego.<|28.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.40|> Forgiveness might be possible and acceptable,<|5.28|> <|5.28|> but forgetting can seem like a step too far,<|8.82|> <|8.82|> making you a doormat or a patsy.<|12.28|> <|13.16|> Self-preservation often precludes the forgetting.<|18.80|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So what does it mean that God is able and willing to forget?<|9.00|> <|9.00|> What can the forgetting engender that simply forgiving cannot?<|15.00|> <|15.00|> For one thing, I don't think God is terribly concerned with God's self-preservation.<|22.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The forgetting allows there to be no keeping score.<|7.00|> <|7.00|> The forgetting allows all barriers to relationship,<|11.00|> <|11.00|> all distractions, to be removed.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> These hopeful words from Jeremiah are stunning.<|5.00|> <|6.94|> Jeremiah, who is known as the weeping prophet,<|11.00|> <|11.00|> who tradition says is the author<|13.52|> <|13.52|> of the Book of Lamentations.<|16.10|> <|16.10|> Jeremiah, who is depicted by Michelangelo<|20.10|> <|20.10|> in the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel,<|22.44|> <|22.44|> and by Rembrandt and Chagall in paintings as a man hunched,<|27.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> forlorn, despondent.<|4.00|> <|4.00|> Jeremiah, who for 29 chapters has earned this way of being depicted,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> suddenly diverges for a few chapters to offer hope and comfort to the Israelites.<|20.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We find today's reading in these chapters called the Book of Consolation or the Book of Comfort,<|9.10|> <|10.36|> a balm so needed by the Israelites who were beating themselves up for what they believed was punishment for disappointing things.<|19.90|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> God. Jeremiah delivers this message of comfort to a people in desperate need of some hope.<|8.98|> <|10.26|> The days are surely coming, the days when the covenant God made with God's people<|15.72|> <|15.72|> will not be written on tablets, which can be broken, or on scrolls, which can be lost or burned.<|24.64|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.62|> A covenant between God and God's people,<|3.54|> <|3.74|> which will be written on their hearts,<|6.10|> <|7.40|> carried within them,<|8.52|> <|9.76|> internalized, and can never be taken away.<|12.70|> <|13.90|> A covenant that will always be accessible to them,<|17.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and needs no intermediary, not Moses, not religious leaders.<|5.86|> <|6.48|> It is given freely to each person.<|9.80|> <|10.86|> And because of this, God's people will not just know God's law.<|15.64|> <|16.48|> They will know God.<|18.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.76|> This hope and comfort delivered by Jeremiah<|4.68|> <|4.68|> is also astonishing for its inclusiveness.<|8.10|> <|8.98|> They shall all know me from the least to the greatest.<|13.98|> <|15.96|> Amy Erickson, my Hebrew Bible professor at ILIF,<|20.96|> <|21.76|> has said that the covenant on the heart<|26.50|> <|26.50|> has a leveling effect.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The internal marker of the covenant binds the community together with an invisible sign that cannot be questioned by genealogy or undermined by accusations of purity.<|15.58|> <|16.80|> Each heart has God's Torah inscribed on it.<|22.28|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> and each of those who have the law written on their hearts, each of those who know God<|6.64|> <|6.64|> will be forgiven, and God will remember their sin no more. My friends, that is some serious good news.<|17.74|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.70|> And if it is shocking to us that God can forgive and forget,<|6.70|> <|8.74|> that gives us some indication of how difficult<|13.32|> <|13.32|> we imagine it would be for mere mortals to do so.<|17.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I wonder.<|3.00|> <|3.00|> I wonder in this last week of Lent,<|8.00|> <|8.00|> this season of so much introspection,<|12.00|> <|12.00|> if we shouldn't spend some time<|16.00|> <|16.00|> sitting with this notion of memory,<|20.00|> <|20.00|> of forgetting.<|23.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> What has God forgiven and forgotten that you are still holding onto?<|10.50|> <|10.50|> What do you think you've done that is too egregious for this gift?<|17.50|> <|17.50|> You still carry it. You still find yourself awake at night turning it over and over.<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> This memory of a thing done or left undone<|5.64|> <|5.64|> that Jeremiah tells us God has not only forgiven, but forgotten.<|12.04|> <|13.80|> What if we were able to set those things down?<|17.98|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.84|> see ourselves, our identity, without this memory<|4.50|> <|4.50|> as a barrier to being fully present to God and others?<|10.24|> <|11.14|> Who would we be?<|12.62|> <|14.62|> Are you still holding on to a wrong done to you?<|18.22|> <|19.12|> Is it one that forgetting would allow you freedom and peace of mind?<|25.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|2.26|> Who might we be if we ceased keeping score?<|7.34|> <|8.80|> What liberty might come with forgetting?<|11.78|> <|15.26|> May you never forget what is worth remembering,<|19.24|> <|20.52|> nor ever remember what is best forgotten.<|24.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.44|> And as my people say, sláinte, health.<|5.94|> <|8.24|> Be well, my friends.<|9.50|> <|10.74|> I miss you.<|12.66|>
